description: This figure presents a dual-axis plot illustrating the effect of phosphorus impurity content on the room-temperature Charpy V-notch impact energy and Brinell hardness of age-hardened Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight high-strength steel (containing ~1 wt.% Si). The x-axis represents phosphorus content ranging from 0 to 0.05 wt.%. The left y-axis displays impact energy in J/cm² (black circular data points with error bars), while the right y-axis shows Brinell Hardness Number (gray square data points with error bars). All specimens were aged for 10 hours at 530°C. The plot reveals a dramatic inverse correlation: as phosphorus content increases from near 0 to 0.045 wt.%, the impact energy plummets from approximately 137 J/cm² to about 25 J/cm²—a reduction exceeding 80%—while the Brinell hardness concurrently increases from roughly 293 to 340. This demonstrates that phosphorus, even in small quantities, severely degrades notch toughness by promoting κ-carbide precipitation along austenite grain boundaries, despite contributing to age-hardening strength. The data underscores the critical need for phosphorus control in Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys intended for high-strain-rate military and transportation applications.
